- **Full Answer**:

- **Answer**: Uncertain Effect

Several large trials found a statistically significant increase in adenoma detection with chromoscopy (e.g. [21159889], [14960519]), and tandem studies found chromoscopy recovered more missed adenomas than repeat conventional inspection [19139000]. Some trials did not show a difference in total adenoma counts but did show increased detection of diminutive/flat lesions with chromoscopy [12196768, 16527699, 16767577], while one high‑definition study showed only a marginal, non‑significant overall increase [20179689]. 

- **Answer**: Higher

In a DM2 population, 80% vs 42.9% were free of AF at 1 year (P = 0.001) favoring CA [18775050]. In persistent AF, CA achieved 70.4% vs 43.7% free of the primary endpoint at 12 months (P = 0.002) and 60.2% vs 29.2% free of any recurrence (>30 s) (P < 0.001) [24135832]. In patients who had failed ADT, ablation ± drugs reduced recurrence (44.1% recurrences vs 91.3% in controls), implying freedom rates of ~55.9% vs ~8.7% at 12 months, favoring ablation (P < 0.001) [16214831].

- **Full Answer**: Catheter ablation yields higher freedom from atrial arrhythmias than anti‑arrhythmic drug therapy: 80% vs 42.9% at 1 year in DM2 patients (P = 0.001) [18775050]; 70.4% vs 43.7% free of the primary 12‑month endpoint and 60.2% vs 29.2% free of any >30 s recurrence in persistent AF (P = 0.002 and P < 0.001, respectively) [24135832]; and ~55.9% vs ~8.7% freedom at 12 months in patients who had failed ADT (recurrence 44.1% vs 91.3%; P < 0.001) [16214831].

- **Answer**: Higher
req-268:
- **Rationale**: A double-blind randomized crossover trial compared plasma exchange (PE) to sham plasma exchange (SPE) using a quantitative neurological disability score (NDS). The primary intention-to-treat analysis found a large, statistically significant improvement in NDS with PE versus sham, indicating greater improvement in disability points with PE.

- **Full Answer**: Plasma exchange produced a greater improvement in disability points: NDS improved by 38 points with PE (P < 0.001) compared with sham exchange, in a randomized double-blind crossover trial [8813270]. The trial design (crossover with washout) and statistical significance support that PE yielded higher disability-scale improvement than sham [8813270].

- **Answer**: Higher

- **Full Answer**: The rate of Caesarean delivery was similar (no difference) when comparing assessment by Bishop score versus transvaginal ultrasound — both trials found no difference in Caesarean rates between groups [15660437; 21484904].

req-270:
- **Rationale**: A randomized double‑blind trial comparing caffeinated (n=568) versus decaffeinated (n=629) instant coffee found no significant difference in mean birth weight; the adjusted mean difference (decaffeinated − caffeinated) was +16 g with a 95% CI of −40 to +73 g, which includes zero, indicating no statistically significant effect [17259189].

- **Full Answer**: In a randomized double‑blind trial of pregnant women drinking ≥3 cups/day, birth weight did not differ significantly between decaffeinated and caffeinated instant coffee groups. After adjustment for gestation length, parity, BMI, and smoking, the decaffeinated group’s infants weighed 16 g more on average than the caffeinated group’s (95% CI −40 to 73 g), a non‑significant difference [17259189].

- **Answer**: No Difference
req-271:
- **Rationale**: Two randomized trials report inconsistent results. One (n=70) found similar all-cause deaths (20 TIPS vs 18 paracentesis), survival probabilities not significantly different (P = 0.51) and concluded TIPS did not improve survival [12454841]. The other (n=60) observed fewer deaths with TIPS (15 vs 23) and higher survival probabilities (69% vs 52% at 1 year), though the unadjusted log-rank comparison did not reach conventional significance (P = 0.11); multivariate analysis favored TIPS for survival without transplantation (P = 0.02) [10841872]. Because results are mixed, sample sizes are small, and statistical significance is inconsistent, the effect on all-cause mortality is not clearly established.

- **Full Answer**: Evidence is inconsistent: one trial found no survival benefit (20 vs 18 deaths; survival difference P = 0.51) [12454841], while another observed fewer deaths with TIPS (15 vs 23) and a non-significant unadjusted survival benefit (P = 0.11) but a favorable multivariate result (P = 0.02) [10841872].

- **Answer**: Uncertain Effect
req-272:
- **Rationale**: Two randomized trials with outcomes in the 6–12 month window reported better visual acuity after vitrectomy versus observation. At 6 months, vitrectomy produced higher rates of hole closure and showed significant improvement on Bailey‑Lovie Word Reading and Potential Acuity Meter tests (and a marginal ETDRS benefit) compared with observation [9006420]. At 12 months (stage 2 holes), vitrectomy resulted in significantly better Word Reading and Potential Acuity Meter acuity than observation (no significant difference on ETDRS or contrast sensitivity) [8644802]. (Longer-term data at 24 months also show greater visual improvement after surgery [14769600].) Note outcomes varied by test and some surgical eyes had vision loss or cataract progression, but overall acuity measures were higher after vitrectomy in the 6–12 month period [9006420][8644802][14769600].

- **Full Answer**: Visual acuity is higher with vitrectomy versus observation at 6–12 months: at 6 months vitrectomy showed significant gains on Bailey‑Lovie Word Reading and Potential Acuity Meter and a marginal ETDRS benefit versus observation [9006420]; at 12 months vitrectomy eyes had significantly better Word Reading and Potential Acuity Meter results than observed eyes [8644802]. (Longer‑term 24‑month data also favor surgery) [14769600].

- **Answer**: Higher
req-273:

- **Full Answer**: Use of written information (interactive booklet) was associated with lower antibiotic use: antibiotics were prescribed to 19.5% of children in the booklet group versus 40.8% in usual care (absolute risk reduction 21.3%, 95% CI 13.7–28.9; P<0.001), with an adjusted odds ratio 0.29 (0.14–0.60) [19640941].

- **Answer**: Lower

req-275:

- **Full Answer**: Part‑time occlusion (2 hours/day) plus any needed spectacles produced larger improvements in amblyopic‑eye visual acuity than spectacles alone: mean improvement at 5 weeks was 1.1 lines for the patching group versus 0.5 lines for spectacles alone (P = 0.006) and mean improvement to best measured VA was 2.2 lines versus 1.3 lines (P < 0.001) [16751033]. A pilot study suggests performing near activities while patched may further increase the patching benefit [15838439].

- **Answer**: Higher
req-276:
- **Rationale**: A randomized trial subgroup analysis found significantly higher 15-year overall survival after postmastectomy radiotherapy (PMRT) versus no RT, with consistent benefit across node subgroups [17306393].

- **Full Answer**: In the DBCG 82 b&c subgroup (node-positive patients with ≥8 nodes removed) 15-year overall survival was 39% with PMRT versus 29% without PMRT (p=0.015) [17306393]. PMRT also improved 15-year survival in patients with 1–3 positive nodes (57% vs 48%, p=0.03) and in those with ≥4 positive nodes (21% vs 12%, p=0.03) [17306393].

- **Answer**: Higher
req-277:
- **Rationale**: A randomized trial comparing families who received active case-management (including eligibility information, application help, liaison with Medicaid/SCHIP) versus traditional outreach/control showed substantially greater insurance enrollment and faster, more continuous coverage in the intervention group.

- **Full Answer**: Children whose families were offered intensive health insurance information and assistance (case managers) were more likely to obtain insurance (96% vs 57%) [16322168]. The intervention produced about an 8-fold increase in adjusted odds of obtaining insurance (OR 7.78; 95% CI 5.20–11.64) [16322168]. Intervention children also had higher continuous coverage (78% vs 30%) and obtained insurance faster (mean 87.5 vs 134.8 days); parents were more satisfied with the process [16322168].

- **Answer**: Higher
req-278:
- **Rationale**: Both included studies directly compared outcomes after cyst aspiration versus conservative management and report no significant difference in IVF outcome measures (including pregnancy). The smaller randomized study found no significant difference in final outcome between aspirated and nonaspirated subgroups [2120087]. The larger prospective series likewise reported that cyst aspiration was not associated with significant differences in oocyte/embryo or pregnancy outcomes and that neither approach improved IVF outcome [16253965].

- **Full Answer**: Clinical pregnancy rate does not differ between ovarian cyst aspiration and conservative management: no significant difference in final pregnancy outcome was found in the randomized study [2120087], and cyst aspiration did not change pregnancy/implantation rates in the larger prospective study [16253965].

- **Answer**: No Difference
req-279:
- **Rationale**: The two randomized studies report conflicting results. One found no significant reduction in Frey's syndrome with SCM flap (and a non‑significant trend toward more objective positives in the flap group) [12464202], while the other found a significant reduction in objective Frey's syndrome with the flap (0/12 vs 6/12 positive) [15871587]. Because findings are inconsistent between trials, the overall effect is uncertain.

- **Full Answer**:
  - Study 12464202 (n=36): symptoms suggestive of Frey's syndrome occurred in 8 flap vs 9 control patients (P=0.31); starch‑iodine test positive in 19 flap vs 11 control patients (P=0.21) — no statistically significant difference [12464202].
  - Study 15871587 (n=24): starch‑iodine test positive in 0/12 flap vs 6/12 control patients (p<0.05), indicating a significant reduction with the sternocleidomastoid flap in that trial [15871587].

- **Answer**: Uncertain Effect
req-280:
- **Rationale**: Both randomized, double‑blind trials compared systemic penicillin to placebo and recorded pain at multiple postoperative time points (up to 72 hours in one study and over a 7‑day diary in the other). Neither study found statistically significant differences in pain (or related measures) between antibiotic and placebo groups at any assessed time point [8734709; 11491635].

- **Full Answer**: Measures of pain were the same (no significant difference) when comparing systemic penicillin versus placebo. Study 8734709 recorded pre‑ and postoperative pain on a visual analog scale for up to 72 hours and found no significant differences between penicillin, placebo, or no‑medication groups (p≥0.05) [8734709]. Study 11491635 used a 7‑day pain diary after endodontic treatment and likewise found that postoperative penicillin did not significantly reduce pain compared with placebo (p>0.05) [11491635].

- **Answer**: No Difference
req-281:
- **Rationale**: Two randomized studies report that adding amlodipine to chelation reduces myocardial iron / increases myocardial T2* over 12 months. A pilot trial found heart T2* rose from 21.7 ± 7.2 ms to 28.3 ± 8.0 ms at 12 months in the amlodipine group versus 26.2 ± 11.4 ms in controls [23830536]. A larger randomized placebo-controlled trial showed amlodipine reduced myocardial iron concentration at 12 months in patients with cardiac siderosis (median change -0.26 mg/g vs 0.01 mg/g; P = .02), consistent with less cardiac iron (i.e., higher T2*) with amlodipine [27412888].

- **Full Answer**: Heart T2* (indicative of less cardiac iron when higher) is higher with amlodipine at 12 months: amlodipine group T2* 28.3 ± 8.0 ms vs control 26.2 ± 11.4 ms at 12 months [23830536]; and amlodipine produced greater reduction in myocardial iron concentration at 12 months versus placebo (median change -0.26 mg/g vs 0.01 mg/g, P = .02) [27412888].

- **Answer**: Higher
req-282:
- **Rationale**: A randomized trial of 72 extremely preterm infants compared re‑feeding gastric residuals to giving fresh feed (i.e., discarding residuals). Time to reach full enteral feeds was 10.0 days with re‑feeding versus 11.3 days with fresh feeding (mean difference −1.3 days), but this difference was not statistically significant (95% CI −2.9 to 0.3; p = 0.11) and the authors conclude re‑feeding does not reduce time to full feeds [25552280].

- **Full Answer**: The trial found a non‑significant 1.3‑day shorter time to full enteral feeding with re‑feeding compared with discarding residuals (10.0 vs 11.3 days; mean difference −1.3 days; 95% CI −2.9 to 0.3; p=0.11), and concluded re‑feeding does not reduce time to achieve full enteral feeding [25552280].

- **Answer**: No Difference
